item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It answers a lot and leaves a lot to mystery, but the vibe is unsettling and interesting +1

item.title: Re: POV
item.content:encoded: thanks for that thoughtful, thought-provoking response! (I have only just now seen it, still learning how to check whether someone has replied a comment)

item.title: Re: POV
item.content:encoded: The effect is a negative for me, but the rest of this article makes up for it. Clear +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the greatest articles on the site I believe. It manages to take one of the most confusing GOIs I ever read through, and turn it into a single concise idea that makes sense, and is also hella cool. I'm gonna find your Author page and read everything that I can now.

definite +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’ve had this line in my idea dump since forever,

GoI of people from previous worlds

and I guess I’m crossing it out now. This article executed it way better than I ever could.

All the +1sss.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand this at all, and I feel like I am close to understanding it, and it's frustrating me to the point where I want help understanding it. could someone probably help? In wikidot PM preferably so I can see it

ty Tufto for helping me. I get it now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A she- I forgot to actually edit it after posting that comment :p have now rectified this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: so is Agent O'Hara a he or a she?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, damn. Finally got around to reading this skip.

Class of 76 tied into SCP-2000? Great idea. Great execution. Tying all of that together with the Marianas Trench docs? Even better. Love the formatting of all of this, love the O5 pov, all of this fits together so well. +1 from me, +2 if I could.

item.title: Syncope Symphony
item.content:encoded: Those people at Syncope Symphony are up to something

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It gives a part of a logical reason for what they did. Not everything is explained, but it's clear that at least part of their thing is bringing back memories of what happened before the document from the Mariana trench was written. We also know that the class of '76 were being experimented on, that removes the mystery of why those things happened to them, gives us part of a reason for that. We have reason to believe that the reason nothing was heard from the Syncope Symphony after '76 was the death of several of their members. We have reason to belief that their members are probably human adjacent.
And that was after one reading, I'm pretty sure I'm going to pick up on some more stuff after another reading.

It doesn't fully explain them, but it gives us a basis of understanding that we did not have before, and while I think the article is well written enough that it has value in its own right, considering it cannon does slightly hurt the other articles in the class of '76, since things about them make some sense now. Because the complete and utter lack of logic in the actions of the Symphony was part of what made them spooky, and that is partly gone now.
I am very tired as a write this, so if I spend a lot of time repeating myself or was unclear, sorry.

I did still +1 this by the way.
Edit: of course any and all information could still be completely false, but assuming that kind of makes fiction pointless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was technically a mistake, but it's one I'll keep, I think- the writer of the note is from a parallel universe where things happened rather differently, after all :p.

item.title: Re: POV
item.content:encoded:
Well, a lot of SCPs (starting rather earlier- Series 3 onwards) have begun to use narrative elements like this a lot more. SCPs are ultimately just pieces of fiction presented in a different format, so there's nothing intrinsically wrong with using framing/narrative devices like this, I think, provided they add something to the story.

Here, I was trying to use the experience of an O5 reading the document to highlight the points about memory and chaos throughout the article. By seeing the document in that very particular perspective, the intent was to build an atmosphere of uncertainty, subjectivity, and isolation in memories to enhance what was going on in the article, as well as creating a stronger narrative arc to push the themes I was going for.

But whether or not it worked, or whether or not that's the effect it actually conveys instead of something else, is entirely up to the reader :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, that was a typo, good spot! Will edit accordingly :p.

I am glad you enjoyed it :).

item.title: POV
item.content:encoded: Hi, new to the site. Well, I've been reading it on and off for years, but I've just made an account and this is my first comment ever. Anyway, what I don't get (and it seems it happens with many post-4000, or maybe even post-3000, SCPs) is the use of out-of-document narration, as if it was a tale. Is this like a new acceptable standard? Or is the text supposed to be really a part of the actual SCP document, and I didn't get that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a question about that in Document 3, Addendum 2, the paragraph of "Anomalous Subject 212", there is "3rd century Triệu dynasty". But in real history, Triệu dynasty began in 207 BC(3rd century BC) and ended in 111 BC. (according to Wikipedia)Is this intended or a mistake?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this article! Quick note:

Agent O'Hara was sent to interview and detain the instance. The following is a log
of his personal camera feed.

Agent O'Hara was found, fully conscious. Upon debriefing, she claimed to have no
recollection of anyone or anything being present in the auditorium.

Just wanted to check if this was on purpose or was a typo. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
tie everything together so neatly and wholly.

Uh… this does nothing of the sort. Neither do those two.

We still don't really know what Syncope is or what it wants. This SCP does not give that answer. Like, at all. It fleshes out Syncope Symphony even more but it absolutely does not provide definitive answers.

Did we even read the same SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is well-written, no doubt, but I've got the same problem with this as I do with the Broken God and Scarlet King -001's. "Things are scarier the less you rationalize them" is Horror 101 and it kills the creep factor to tie everything together so neatly and wholly. All these things individually were so much more interesting when they were only addressed indirectly and no attempt was made to explain them.

Like, no other CO76 article even acknowledges (besides in hyperlinks) that there's anything tying these mysteries together. I like not being able to get a straight answer, I like not knowing what Syncope is or what it wants.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is simply brilliant. Writing Syncope Symphony as an SCP, and coming up with something resembling an explanation and justification for the Class of '76… These are two ideas that would be so easy to mess up individually. You totally succeed at combining both of them. Connecting the Class with SCP-2000 is genius, it works so well, and somehow manages to explain the Class without diminishing it in the process.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, you wouldn't think that the two flavors would work together, but they do! Really well! Good job Tufto. It's hard to find preexisting things on this site and blend them together as well as this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit someone wrote the Syncope Symphony into a thing. Oh my god. Wow. Nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is, overall, a very good piece. You're poignant where it counts, but the logs are too pretty in places, and there's some tidying up of prose that's possible- sent you a few IRC .tells about it. Upvoted in general.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't need this existential dread today. Still great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love, love, love it!

Class of '76 stuff always just sucks me in. The mixture of memory, regrets, childhood.

So good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As stated before, I love these types of scps that break the format justttt right with the POV.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An excellent edition to the Class of ‘76 canon. The connection between the nostalgia based horror of Co76 and the “Remember us” line of Document Recovered From the Mariana Trench and SCP-2000 is brilliant. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tufto youve got me feeling some sort of way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A long long time ago
I can still remember how
That music used to make me smile
And I knew if I had my chance
That I could make those people dance
And maybe they'd be happy for a while
But February made me shiver
With every paper I'd deliver
Bad news on the doorstep
I couldn't take one more step
I can't remember if I cried
When I read about his widowed bride
Something touched me deep inside
The day the music died

With thanks to the following:

Rounder "Yurt Boi" House
Stallmantic
DrMartens
Woedenaz
KindlyTurtleClem

This skip messes heavily with Roget's Class of 76 canon. If he'd like me to take it down I will do so at once.

The first image belongs to Woedenaz, and is used with their kind permission.
The second image is from here, is CC-compliant, and belongs to JR Korpa.
